sentences.text: As far as I am concerned, this was the worst that ever happened in Plaszow, regardless of all the killings and then hanging because-- because of the little children who were yelling and screaming and crying on those trucks.
But some of the children had courage enough to run and  hide.
And I had a little cousin, a girl who was about 11 years old, who when she saw what was going on, she ran into the latrine and jumped into one of the holes in the latrine and hid there.
After the whole thing was over, and people came back to the barracks, some of the women to the latrine.
And they noticed a little girl.
And they knew that my mother is a relative.
They ran to my mother and told her about it.
My mother came out and took her out of the-- of the hole, from the latrine.
And she is alive today, lives in New Jersey.
Well her mother worked as a cook for a German in a certain little camp outside of Plaszow.
And her mother arranged to the German to bring her into that camp.
And she-- and she was a big girl.
And she kept her throughout the war like this.
And she survived.
Another unpleasant occurrence that I had in Plaszow was when I worked for Madritsch and we were all standing to be counted at the Appellplatz.
And there was a deputy commander of the Jewish police by the name of Finkelstein, who went through the group.
And my mother was standing, crouch or whatever, and he used profanity on my mother.
As he was counting, when he came to me, I said, you know, I don't like the fact that you used profanity on my mother.
He says, you don't?
Come with me.
And he took me to a bunker, which was in the basement of a prison in Plaszow.
There were cells, which were six feet tall and three feet square.
And you could only stand in that bunker.
You couldn't sit down.
He gave me 72 hours, which went three full days.
And I was getting, through a little window, one meal a day.
But this meal was not that important as it was that I couldn't relieve myself.
I had to relieve myself right there on-- in that bunker.
And that was the worst.
And there was no washing or any hygiene to be had-- nothing.
I spend the 72 hours-- there was a woman, polish woman, gentile woman, in the next bunker.
And she was singing all the time.
And I was singing with her.
We actually were singing for 72 hours.
And when I came out the sunlight hit my eyes and I couldn't-- I couldn't see for a couple of hours, because I was standing in this dark, dingy bunker.
Now this was a Jewish policeman who put me in there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
The next thing that happened, while I was there, is that my uncle, my mother's brother, worked also in a group that was outside of the-- of Plaszow.
They lived in the factory, where they worked.
And there were about 120 people.
And news came that they brought those people to camp and they going to be shot.
There was a little hill in Plaszow called Hujowa Gorka, which was-- I have to use profanity.
Can I?
It was called the Prick Hill.
That's how they gave the name to it.
They even describe it in certain books about Plaszow.
And this was the hill where they were usually executing people.
So when I heard that my uncle's group came in, I-- a friend of ours, a young girl, was working right near this Hujowa Gorka.
And I ask her whether I could hide in her little shack, facing it, because I want to see whether my uncle is in that group.
Now I had to watch execution of 120 people, among them two brothers of which one of the brothers was a schoolmate of mine, and their mother.
My uncle was not in that group.
I don't know what happened to him, but he was not.
And I had to watch the complete execution, which was done by Latvian guards.
It was a young Latvian commander, who was a university student, who was giving the orders to shoot, whose name was Ianets.
I'll never forget the name.
I also won't forget the face.
And they made the people strip.
They put them against the hill.
And they were going around with machine guns and killing.
Finally, in the middle of the execution, I just couldn't watch anymore.
And I left the place.
And I came down to camp.
And I told my mother, you know, your brother wasn't there.
I don't know what happened.
Three days later, my uncle shows up in camp.
I say, what happened to you?
He said, when I saw what was going on, I jumped out of the-- he was a little man, thin, short, not very energetic.
I would never expect it of him.
He says he jumped out the window and he ran.
He didn't know where to go.
He went to a woman that worked with him.
My uncle was a bank executive before the war.
He ran to the woman, who work in his department-- a polish woman.
She kept him for two days.
She says, I cannot keep you.
I don't want to take the chances.
So he went in front of the camp.
And it so happened that a group was coming, from which one prisoner escaped.
And they put them into that group.
Because you know what happened?
When a prisoner escaped, and a group came in, and they counted, and one was missing, they selected 10 or 12 to be shot.
For every escapee, 10 or 12 people were shot.
So my uncle got into the group.
And he came to camp.
He finally went through Buchenwald.
He survived.
He lives now in California.
But with him was his brother-in-law, in that camp.
And he ran.
And my uncle by chance didn't meet his brother-in-law until 1970.
His brother-in-law lived in Pittsburgh.
Now this was-- this was it.
So I work at Madrich.
Finally, it was 1943 and the Russian troops were getting closer to Plaszow.
And a rumor started that the Germans are going to liquidate the camp.
And the liquidation meant death.
So we all didn't know.
We were making plans.
Maybe we could escape.
Maybe we can do it that way or that way.
In the meantime, we hear that Schindler is moving his factory and he's taking 12-- 1,100 or 1,200 people with him.
Well Madritsch-- Julius Madritsch-- and his deputy Raymond Titsch, who was also Austrian, were really good men.
I must say that they-- they were bringing us food to Plaszow and-- and they were hiding-- Raymond Titsch hid some of my father's jewelry that my father gave him to hide.
And he returned after the war.
And he was a poor man in Vienna.
And Madritsch went to Schindler and said, look, you're making a list.
I want you to take 200 of my people.
He says, I cannot take 200 people.
Because I have so many people of my own.
Finally, they agree that he'll take 80 people from Madritsch group on Schindler's list.
And that's how my father, my mother, and my sister-- my sister already, for about two years, had a back problem.
It was either a slipped disk or whatever.
She just couldn't walk.
And whenever there were Aktions in camp, we used to help her.
She used to stand-- in pain, she was walking, in order the German shouldn't see that she-- she is crippled that way.
So we all got on the Schindler's list
Well as I told you, my father-- my father was very close to Madritsch.
And we were one of the first to be on the list.
He put in-- first of all, his favorites he put on the list, and then others.
So when the time came to leave, they put us into boxcars-- women separately and men separately.
We didn't know where we're going or what it's going to be.
All we knew that we were on Schindler's list.
And we  thought that this is the best thing that could happen to us.
But we still didn't trust that Schindler would persevere.
Well I knew that he was tolerant, that he didn't let anybody to-- to abuse his workers.
And this was good enough.
I mean the-- in those days he was like-- Madritsch did the same thing, Julius Madritsch and Raymond Titsch.
We were just lucky that we fall into those hands.
They put us into boxcars and we started going.
We didn't know where we were going.
All of a sudden, after one and a half days of traveling in the boxcar, on a very cold day in October, we arrive someplace.
We didn't know where we were.
We came in.
And there was a band-- prisoners band standing at the railroad station.
They were playing.
And we got out of the boxcars.
And some of the kapos where there, and guards.
And they said, you are in the Gross Rosen concentration camp.
We knew by reputation of Gross Rosen is extermination camp.
But we didn't know why we wound up in Gross Rosen.
We had no idea.
They took us to an Appellplatz in Gross Rosen.
And they ask us to strip completely.
And this was  end of October.
It must have been-- this was in an area that was very cold.
It must have been like 20 degrees.
And we had to strip completely.
They took away our clothing, shoes, everything.
And they ran us to a tent, into a tent.
All 350 of us, or 400 of us, they put into a tent.
No, we were more.
We were like 700 or 800 of us-- 700 I think. 700.
They put us into a tent.
And we were squeezed, one on top the other.
And my father was with me.
And from lack of air, he fainted on me.
So I fought through to get him a little bit into the-- outside into air, close to the opening in the tent and he finally came to.
From the tent, they ran us to showers.
No, excuse me.
First they ran us to-- to a sort of barbershop, where the Ukrainian-- they were not barbers, but they were shaving us.
They shaved us all over-- our legs, all over bodies.
And while they were doing, they were cutting us.
And they were doing it on purpose.
Then they ran us into the showers.
And they gave us some kind of a compound to wash with, which was not soap.
But once we put it on, on the wounds that we had, it wasn't burning.
And we had heard about showers.
And we were apprehensive to see what's going to happen.
And when the water came out, it was the greatest of reliefs.
From the showers, they ran us naked and wet into a barrack, where they were throwing clothing at us-- shoes and stripe suits-- disregarding the size or whatever.
And then they took us to another barrack.
And we were putting on this clothing.
And I had pants that I couldn't close they were so small.
They were like shorts.
My shoes were too small.
But it was close to the night already.
And we were all going to sleep.
And how did we sleep?
The barrack was for 200 people.
And they put in 700 people there.
And we were all sitting with spread eagle, into each other.
And this is how we spent the first night, the second night, the third night, the fourth night, the fifth night.
But at night, while people were asleep, we used to walk over bodies and start exchanging the clothing.
So on the fourth, fifth day, everybody had shoes his own size.
Because if I swiped somebody's shoes that were larger, and somebody got my shoes that needed a smaller size-- so eventually it equalized.
During the day, they made us-- our barrack was right across the street from the crematorium, which was intimidating.
During the day, we were standing outside, just doing nothing.
And if somebody wanted to relieve himself, had had to report.
And if they said no, he couldn't go.
And if he had to run, without asking, one got beaten.
All of a sudden, we hear that Schindler arranged for us to go to Brunnlitz.
And one day, they load us again into boxcars and we are transported to Brunnlitz, to Schindler's camp.
And as we arrive in Brunnlitz, we walked out of the boxcars, Schindler was standing there.
We felt such a relief that here is the man who is going to protect us.
And we felt that maybe, maybe now we have a chance of survival.
The camp was not a camp really.
It was a factory.
Downstairs was the factory.
Upstairs we slept.
We slept on hay, on the floor.
But we started worrying about women.
Where is my mother?
Where's my sister?
Where are the women?
And a week went by, and two weeks went by, and the women aren't there.
And then we found out that Schindler sent somebody to Auschwitz to get the women.
And the women are still not there, until he himself went to Auschwitz, and he brought them out of Auschwitz.
It was another relief when the women came.
We had SS guards with a camp commander by the name of Leipold, who was a beast.
But Schindler kept him in line.
He was afraid of Schindler because Schindler had his connections.
And whenever Schindler left, on business or whatever, Leipold took advantage of it.
At one time, there were a few children there in camp.
Leipold sent them to Auschwitz while Schindler was away.
Now the factory-- the factory he brought all the machinery from Poland.
We set up the machinery.
I was put into a tool making department.
What did I know about tool making?
Nothing.
My sister came.
And she was much worse.
She couldn't walk.
So they put her into the infirmary there.
And while we were in the factory, I was working on tools.
Nothing ever was manufactured in the factory.
But for appearances, we were working.
Food was scarce.
And my sister was deteriorating at the infirmary.
So one day I stopped Mrs.
Schindler and I ask her, could you provide some more food for my sister in the infirmary?
And she took the name.
And from then on, my sister was getting farina and apples every day in the infirmary.
One day I was running down into the men's room, while I was sleeping, and I fell down the staircase and I broke my glasses.
And I couldn't see without my glasses while I work.
I had another pair of glasses with a Polish family in Krakow that kept a lot of things.
So they had our better clothing, my mother's fur coats, and some of the Persian rugs, and also a reserve pair of glasses.
I stopped Mrs.
Schindler on the factory floor and I said, Mrs.
Schindler, would you excuse me.
I broke my glasses.
I have another pair with a Polish family in Krakow.
If you would know anybody who travels-- this was Czechoslovakia, mind you-- who travels to Krakow, I would appreciate if maybe they could get my pair of glasses.
She says, give me the address.
I said, tomorrow I'll provide you the address.
And the next day, she was walking through the factory, I gave her a piece of paper with the address and the name of the people.
About 10 days later, I'd had my glasses.
Now this was-- if you were not there, you don't realize what it meant to get a pair of glasses in Czechoslovakia from Poland, what Mrs.
Schindler arranged for.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Now we were there for a while.

And a friend of mine, who was from my school, and was very close to Schindler, because he-- he was Schindler's chauffeur, told to me that they have a group they created underground, that a Czech underground was providing weapons for them-- whether I want to join.
I said, yes I do.
He said, OK.
He talked to Schindler about me.
Schindler knew all about it.
And Schindler put me into electrical transformer, where there were huge bails of electrical wires.
And under those wires, they were hiding the weapons and ammunition.
And my job was to make sure that the German foreman in the transformer would not get close to it.
But every time he got close to it, my heart was stopping.
And the whole thing took a couple of weeks that I was there.

But the greatest thing that Schindler did is when the news came-- and this was around February, the beginning of February of 1945-- that some boxcars were abandoned on a siding, four boxcars with Jews.
And this was a very severe winter.
Schindler went by himself.
And he found out that there were Jewish people there.
He came back.
And he dispatched two trucks with people-- I was among them-- in order to open the boxcars and get the people out.
The doors of those boxcars were frozen, completely frozen.
And we couldn't open it.
So he sent back one truck to bring torches.
And we heat up with torches those doors and we opened.
Now I would say that 80% of those people were dead by that time.
And the 20% that were alive were walking skeletons.
There was a Jewish doctor with us.
And he checked the pulse.
And whoever was alive, put on the truck and we brought back to camp.
And Schindler set up a hospital for them.
He acquired cots.
He went and bought on black market food for them-- milk, and oatmeal, and farina, and all kinds of things.
And there were like 80 of them.
And he brought them back to life.
They were Hungarian Jews.
And by the time the war was over, they were already functioning.
They were still very thin, but they were functioning.
In the meantime, my sister was getting worse and worse because she was getting-- what do you call it from lying in bed, sores?
Bed sores.
Bed sores.
And the doctor in charge of the infirmary, who was a Jewish doctor from Krakow, decided that she probably-- they couldn't diagnose her condition, really.
And I never believed that she had cancer.
He decided she probably had cancer.
And it was like 10, 12 days before end of the war, he gave her an injection, which killed her.
Schindler arranged for my father and myself to bury her.
And one of the Jewish carpenters made a casket, a very rough type of a casket.
And we were dispatched on a wagon with a horse and a German guard to some of the woods to bury her, bury the casket.
And we took shovels with us.
And we made a hole.
And we buried the casket.
But we-- we made sure of the location.
And we counted the steps from the road.
And 12 days or two weeks later, the war was over.
And I can't forgive the doctor until today.
Because if he would let her live those 12 days, all we were-- we had on our mind is to bring her to the West and get proper medical care.
She was-- at that time, she was only 23 years old.
Anyway, after the war, when the war was over, and we brought the casket up and we buried her in Prague, in a Jewish cemetery in Prague.
And from time to time, I visit there.
And my son goes and visits.
So she is buried in the same cemetery that Kafka is buried.
You know, I met Schindler several times.
I met him-- in fact, right after the war I was in his house in Reg-- he lived in Regensburg, Germany, in Bavaria.
And I spent a whole night drinking with him.
Because the only way I could get something out of it is drinking.
And he was-- he could drink two bottles of whiskey.
And I ask him, tell me, why did you do it?
And he gave me a story that a rabbi lived next-- next door.
And he always had problems with his father.
And whenever he had a problem with his father, he used to go to the rabbi's house.
And the rabbi had a son his own age.
And the rabbi used to quiet-- the rabbi used to quiet him down and just don't worry-- just disregard it.
And he says he never-- he could never forget it.
My speculation is that Schindler came as a black marketeer to Poland.
And his only aim was to make money, in which he was helped by Abraham Bankier who was the head of the-- the owner of the Emalia factory.
He got to like those few Jews who were around him.
And he said, why are-- why are they killing them?
They're not such bad people.
Besides, he was a man who loved life.
He was a bon vivant.
He loved women, wine.
He could drink.
He could party.
He-- he loved life.
And after two, three years, it became like a sport for him.
Maybe he'll defy the final solution.
Maybe he'll-- he'll beat the system and he'll-- he'll save-- it was a like a-- he'll save those 1,100 Jews.
And at the end of the war, he has seen that the war is coming to an end.
And he was on the list of war criminals in Czechoslovakia, because he was an original Nazi in Czechoslovakia.
So he felt that he-- he will rehabilitate himself this way.
But basically, I think he was a good man.
And he-- he wanted to-- to make sure that he'll save all those families together, which he did.
We visited Schindler several times Frankfurt after the war.
And I talked to him many times.
And he lived with the war.
He couldn't function in normal circumstances.
As brave as he was during the war and enterprising, he just couldn't function in normal world.
He couldn't make a living.
He lived in a very poor circumstances in-- across the street from a railroad station.
In Europe, in every city, the railroad station is always in the worst neighborhood.
And he lived across the street, in a dingy third-floor apartment.
And it was really a pity.
He was always dapper.
Whenever he was helped with money, he spent it fast, because he loved women and drinking.
He left his wife in Argentina.
And he left her there.
And he moved to Germany.
But he lived with the war times, with the people he saved.
He knew about everybody's children's bar mitzvah.
And he was telling me that this one has a son.
He has so much [INAUDIBLE] from him.
And this one is this.
And this one is that.
He was always telling me stories about the people whom he saved.
And he was entertained every single year by the Israeli government.
He was invited to Israel.
And they used to throw a banquet for him and entertain him.
And he was a big hero in Israel, and rightly so.
He was the only one who really saved 1,100 people.
